coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots embedded in solid-state optically transparent matrices
coredata.dc:creator: Kiryakov, Arseny
Kuznetsova, Yulia
Sivaganesh, Dhanushkodi
Dyachkova, Tatiana
Aleksandr, Chufarov
Murugan, Jeevaraj
Tyutyunnik, Alexander
coredata.dc:description: For the first time, MgAl2O4 nanoceramics functionalized with carbon dots were prepared by thermobaric synthesis. A comprehensive characterization of the obtained MgAl2O4: C nanocomposites is performed using XRD, SEM, XPS and Raman spectroscopy. It is shown that the technological modes of synthesis used make it possible to minimize the interaction of carbon with the oxide sublattice of the solid-state host and promote the stabilization of carbon dots. The types of optical centers and the nature of photoluminescence are determined by optical spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectroscopy with stationary and laser excitation. The synthesized MgAl2O4: С nanocomposites are transparent in visible spectral range, and absorption in the UV spectral range is associated with optical transitions both in the intrinsic defects of host (F-centers) and in carbon dots. It was shown that excitation-dependent luminescence is associated with continuous energy states of defects on the surface of carbon dots. The results obtained indicate the possibility of synthesizing optical nanoceramics with stable carbon dots distributed throughout the volume and having weak interaction with the host.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanoceramics
Nanocomposites
Aluminum-magnesium spinel
Thermobaric synthesis
Optical absorption
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of electronic structure, vibrational and optical properties

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ural Branch, Russian Academy of Sciences
Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology
Institute of Solid State Chemistry of the Ural Branch of the Russian Academy of Sciences

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Russian Federation
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education and Science of the Russian Federation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the Russian Science Foundation grant No. 23-72-01024, https://rscf.ru/project/23–72–01024/ and from the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Russian Federation (Ural Federal University Program of Development within the Priority-2030 Program) as well as the state assignment for the Institute of Solid State Chemistry of the Ural Brunch of Russian Academy of Sciences (АААА-А19-119031890025-9). The authors thank V.A. Volkovich for FTIR spectroscopy; the UCSU "Modern Nanotechnologies" (Institute of Natural Sciences and Mathematics, UrFU) for XPS measurements; the "Metalab" laboratory (Institute of Physics and Technology, UrFU) for access to spectroscopic equipment; Ju.V.Shchapova and Center for Collective Use Geoanalyst IGG UB RAS for Raman investigation.

The work was supported by the Russian Science Foundation grant No. 23-72-01024 , https://rscf.ru/project/23–72–01024/ and from the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Russian Federation (Ural Federal University Program of Development within the Priority-2030 Program) as well as the state assignment for the Institute of Solid State Chemistry of the Ural Brunch of Russian Academy of Sciences (АААА-А19-119031890025-9).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction pattern of MgAl2O4 nanoceramics with an admixture of carbon nanopowder. Each phase is highlighted in the corresponding color. A photograph of the synthesized composite is shown in the upper right corner.
The SEM images of the surface of initial carbon nanopowder, pure MgAl2O4 nanoceramic, and MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite.

(a) XPS survey spectra of pure MgAl2O4 nanoceramic as reference and MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite; (b) C 1s core-level spectrum; (c) O 1s core-level spectra; (d) Al 2p core-level spectra compared to native AlxOy – Al-metal; (e) Mg 1s core-level spectra compared to MgO – Mg-metal; (f) Valence band spectra.
Raman spectrum of MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite.

(a) Optical absorption spectra of MgAl2O4: C composites with different content of carbon. The dashed lines show the exponential baselines; (b) A spectral analysis of UV absorption band derived as the difference between the absorption spectra and exponential baseline. The arrows show the bands corresponding to optical transitions in intrinsic defects of host (F- and F+ - centers) and CDs.
Excitation spectra of various photoluminescence bands for MgAl2O4:С nanocomposite.
Photoluminescence spectra of MgAl2O4:C nanocomposite at different excitation energies.
Decay kinetic of CDs emission at 2.21 eV under pulsed laser excitation at 3 eV.
Phase composition and corresponding crystallographic parameters of MgAl2O4:C nanocomposite.
The nature of vibrational modes in MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite.

row.entry: sp2 -hybridized C in rings containing seven or more atoms
sp2 -hybridized C on the armchair and zigzag edges of graphene

head.ce:title: Carbon dots embedded in solid-state optically transparent matrices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Physics, Thiagrajar college of Engineering, Madurai, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the first time, MgAl2O4 nanoceramics functionalized with carbon dots were prepared by thermobaric synthesis. A comprehensive characterization of the obtained MgAl2O4: C nanocomposites is performed using XRD, SEM, XPS and Raman spectroscopy. It is shown that the technological modes of synthesis used make it possible to minimize the interaction of carbon with the oxide sublattice of the solid-state host and promote the stabilization of carbon dots. The types of optical centers and the nature of photoluminescence are determined by optical spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectroscopy with stationary and laser excitation. The synthesized MgAl2O4: С nanocomposites are transparent in visible spectral range, and absorption in the UV spectral range is associated with optical transitions both in the intrinsic defects of host (F-centers) and in carbon dots. It was shown that excitation-dependent luminescence is associated with continuous energy states of defects on the surface of carbon dots. The results obtained indicate the possibility of synthesizing optical nanoceramics with stable carbon dots distributed throughout the volume and having weak interaction with the host.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, the development of new light-emitting materials based on 0D nanostructures (quantum dots – QDs) has been the subject of intense research. The quantum-confinement effect provides a number of unique properties of QDs, among which the following can be distinguished: tuning the band gap and the spectral composition of the luminescence by varying the size of the QDs [1]; a large value of the Stokes shift, which reduces the probability of self-quenching of the emission [2]; high photostability, which allows the use of high-power excitation sources [3]. These features of QDs determine the possibilities of their practical use in sensors [4], LEDs [5], lasers [6], solar cells [7], biological markers [8], quantum information systems [9], etc. Significant progress has been made in the development of high-performance displays and LEDs based on colloidal semiconductor quantum dots, such as CdSe, CdTe, and PbTe [10,11]. However, toxic and harmful effects on humans and the environment are crucial obstacles to the use of Cd- and Pb-based QDs for the development of next-generation phosphors.

As an alternative to Cd- and Pb-based QDs, carbon dots (CDs) aroused great interest due to their low chemical toxicity, cost-effective preparation, bright and tunable photoluminescence, and biocompatibility [12–14]. The complex of these properties allows the use of CDs not only for lighting technologies, but also for biomedicine [15]. In general, the photoluminescent properties of CDs are determined by radiative transitions both in the carbon core and in the surface functional groups. Luminescence of CDs can be adjusted by varying the size of CDs, structure (graphite, diamond, amorphous carbon) and morphology of core and changing the type of functional surface groups [16–19]. An increase in the size of the CDs leads to a decrease in the HOMO-LUMO energy gap and to a red shift of luminescence [20]. In general, the higher crystallinity indicates that the CDs have a relatively complete sp2-conjugate structure, which provides a high luminescence quantum yield [21]. In addition, the structure of CDs affects not only the luminescence intensity, but also its spectral range. For example, in the work [22] it has been shown that when the sp2-structure is destroyed, the emission wavelength is blue-shifted. In addition to radiative electron-hole recombination in the carbon core, the luminescence of CDs can also be associated with optical transitions between local electronic states of surface groups. Surface defects are mainly formed as a result of surface oxidation, and the higher the degree of surface oxidation of CDs, the greater the redshift of the emission wavelength [23,24]. In addition, it is possible to functionalize the surface of CDs with fluorescent organic molecules that provide an intense emission in the visible spectrum range (from blue to red, depending on the type of molecules used) [25,26].

In most cases, the synthesis of CDs is carried out in liquid media by chemical and physico-chemical methods. Integration of CDs into solid-state systems is possible by creating surface coatings when spraying solutions with CDs [27] or by implantation with carbon ions [28]. In turn, obtaining CDs in the volume of solid-state matrices is still an important technological problem. One of the features is that in order to effectively use CDs, a solid-state matrix could have a wide range of optical transparency. Promising candidates in this case are Al2O3, SiO2, MgO, MgAl2O4, having a band gap of more than 7 eV. In these oxide systems, the temperatures of consolidation (for ceramics) or melting (for glasses or single crystals) are high. The problem also lies in the interaction of carbon and oxygen in oxide materials, at high synthesis temperatures of composites. In this regard, it is extremely difficult to functionalize the volume of the desired optically transparent material without destroying the CDs.

The purpose of this work is to functionalize optically transparent MgAl2O4 nanoceramics by CDs using the original thermobaric synthesis technology. For the first time, we synthesized optically transparent nanocomposite from a mixture of MgAl2O4 and carbon nanopowders. A comprehensive characterization of the obtained material was performed using X-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy. The types of optical centers and the nature of photoluminescence in a wide spectral range have been determined by optical spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectroscopy with stationary and laser excitation.

Aluminum-magnesium spinel nanopowder (MgAl2O4) was synthesized by chemical precipitation from solutions of aluminum and magnesium nitrates. The average size of the coherent scattering region for obtained nanopowder was determined to be 20 nm in accordance with the X-ray diffraction full-profile analysis. The MgAl2O4 nanopowder was further mixed with carbon nanopowder (RUS GRAPHENE LLC) in a sapphire mortar for an hour. Two compositions were prepared with the ratio of spinel nanopowder to carbon nanopowder 10000:1 and 10000:10, respectively. The spinel nanopowder was previously degassed for 3 h at 500 °C. The resulting mixture was subjected to thermobaric action at a pressure of 6 GPa and a temperature of 600 °C for 10 minutes. The features of the thermobaric synthesis of transparent spinel nanoceramics were described in detail in previous works [29–31].

X-ray diffraction phase analysis of the initial nanopowders as well as obtained MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite was performed on a STADI-P diffractometer (Stoe) equipped with a linear mini PSD detector using CuKa1 radiation in the range of 2θ from 5° to 120° with a step of 0.02°. Polycrystalline silicon (a = 5.43075 Å) was used as an external standard. The database of powder standards PDF2 (ICDD, USA, issue 2016) was used to determine possible impurity phases. The unit cell parameters and mass fractions of the detected phases were refined by the Rietveld method using GSAS software [32,33].

X-ray diffraction pattern of MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 1 . The synthesized composite contains a mixture of phases of aluminum-magnesium spinel (MgAl2O4, cubic structure) and graphite modification of carbon (C-graphite), as listed in Table 1 . In addition, small inclusions of the α-Al2O3 phase and metallic platinum have also been found. The presence of platinum is explained by inclusions of platinum foil, which was used in thermobaric synthesis as an inert shell of the compact. Traces of the aluminum oxide phase can be caused either by components that have not fully reacted during the synthesis of the spinel nanopowder, or by contamination during mixing of carbon nanopowder and spinel nanopowder in a mortar with a sapphire pestle. X-ray diffraction patterns and data obtained by the Rietveld method for the initial carbon nanopowder and MgAl2O4 ceramics without carbon additives (for comparison) are given in Supplementary Materials (S1-S4).

The surface morphology was studied using a SIGMA VP scanning electron microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany). The SEM images of the initial carbon nanopowder surface, pure MgAl2O4 nanoceramic, and MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 2 . The carbon nanopowder is represented by carbon plates forming a stack. At high resolution, it can be seen that some sections of plates are transparent to the scanning electron beam because of low (possibly monolayer) number of carbon sheets. The surface of pure MgAl2O4 nanoceramic is sintered nanoparticles forming a dense agglomerate. The thermobaric conditions of synthesis are sufficient to displace large pores. Pressure relief and temperature reduction doesn't lead to the formation of cracks. The MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite is also characterized by the absence of cracks and large pores. The surface of the composite is a sintered agglomerate of aluminum-magnesium spinel nanoparticles. Inclusions caused by the presence of carbon plates (possibly in couple of graphene sheets) are found on the surface of the composite. The size of carbon inclusions varies greatly from 200 nm to 800 nm. Additional SEM images of the MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite are given in Supplementary Materials (S7).

Confirmation of the morphology of nanograins, as well as the morphology of quantum dots, was carried out using HRSEM using JEOL-2100+ High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope Accelerating Voltage: 200 kV Resolution: Point: 0.194 nm, Lattice: 0.14 nm, 5-axis eucentric goniometer stage, Specimen Tilt: ±60°, SAED, Tomography, STEM, BF and DF images (Supplementary Materials S8-10). The micrographs of the morphology in Figures S8(a-c) show 20 nm size matrix particles contacting each other through disordered interfaces near the 1–2 nm thick grain surface.

The polycrystalline nature of cubic MgAl2O4 becomes evident through the distinctive co-centric rings captured in the selective area electron diffraction pattern, clearly depicted in Figure S9. These concentric rings, starting from the outermost, delineate specific d-spacing values of 2.90, 2.42, 2.01, 1.42, 1.10, 1.04, 0.92, and 0.90 Å, corresponding to the crystallographic planes (840), (751), (800), (731), (511), (400), (311), and (220), respectively. These findings clearly affirm the polycrystalline character of cubic MgAl2O4 and also exhibit a remarkable alignment with the data obtained through XRD, succinctly summarized in Table S10. The congruence between the electron diffraction and XRD results solidifies our understanding of the material's structural composition, reinforcing its cubic nature and crystallographic arrangement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of electronic structure, vibrational and optical properties
ce:section.ce:para: XPS K-Alphaþ™ spectrometer after ThermoScientific™ (Compact form-factor, Research&Development Class of equipment) was employed to study the electronic structure. This system uses an AlKa microfocused X-ray gun (1486.7 eV) with a probe with a diameter of 300 mm and an X-ray power of no more than 72 W supplied to the sample. Standard depth output of XPS signal (outcoming photoelectrons) is not less than 8 nm.
Raman spectra were recorded using a confocal spectrometer LabRam HR800 Evolution (Horiba, Japan) under laser excitation at 488 nm (2.54 eV).

Optical absorption spectra were obtained at room temperature on a Perkin Elmer lambda 35 spectrophotometer equipped with a halogen lamp (1100 – 350 nm / 1.13–3.54 eV) and a deuterium lamp (350 – 190 nm / 3.54–6.52 eV). The samples were not pre-treated with polishing or other means to ensure transparency. The concentration of optically active centers N was estimated using the Smakula-Dexter equation [34]:
(1)
N = 8.7 ·
10 16
n 0
(
n 0
2
+ 2 )
2
·
Δ
E
f
·
α 0
,
where
n 0
is the refractive index at the wavelength of the maximum of the absorption band,
Δ
E
is the width of the absorption band determined at half its height, f is the oscillator strength of the optical transition,
α 0
is the absorption coefficient at the maximum of the absorption band (it was obtained by converting the optical density (D) through the dependence
α = 2.3 D / d
, where d is the thickness of the sample equal to 0.28 mm). The refractive index for aluminum-magnesium spinel was calculated using the following equation [35]:
(2)
n 2
= 1 + 1.8938
λ 2
λ 2
−
0.09942
2
+ 3.0755
λ 2
λ 2
−
15.826
2
,
where λ is the wavelength, in µm. Since the exact value of the oscillator strength for optical transitions in the materials under study is unknown, we used the value f = 1. In this case, the calculated N values represent the lower limit of the actual concentrations of optically active centers.

Photoluminescence spectra were obtained using a Fluorolog – 3 spectrofluorimeter (Horiba, Japan) equipped with a 450 W xenon lamp and a Synapse plus CCD camera (Horiba). The kinetic parameters of photoluminescence were measured by switching a xenon lamp to picosecond laser (HoribaTM) using a multi-channel PPD detector.

Fig. 3 (a) shows survey XPS spectra of pure MgAl2O4 nanoceramic (as reference) and MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite. From this figure it's well seen that both XPS survey spectra exhibit well-resolved sharp XPS peaks related to the appropriate electronic states of Mg, Al, O and C. The origin of XPS peaks was identified with the use of [36–39]. No XPS signals from contaminators are seen in these spectra at 0.05 at.% element sensitivity limit of employed XPS spectrometer thus proving high-purity of MgAl2O4: C sample. XPS Elemental Concentration Analysis gives the concentration value of the doped carbon in MgAl2O4: C sample as 43.2 at.%.

XPS C 1s core-level spectrum shown in Fig. 3(b) is non-elementary. The main peak located at 284.05 eV originates from C–C chemical bond which has sp2 hybridization [37,39]. A less intense peak at 285.00 eV is associated with sp3 hybridized carbon atoms [40,41]. This indicates the formation of carbon inclusions with graphene (mainly) and diamond structures in MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite. The formation of diamond structure in carbon-doped nanoceramic is possible due to thermobaric synthesis technology. In general, the temperature used in the synthesis (600 °C) should not ensure the formation of C–C chemical bond which has sp3 hybridization. However, the pressure (6 GPa) is sufficient for the synthesis of diamond-like structures [42]. We assume that the compression of carbon particles in the oxide matrix follows the mechanism of interaction of individual oxide crystallites between which there are carbon inclusions (by analogy with a hammer and an anvil). Local pressures in this case can significantly exceed the synthesis pressure of nanoceramics and stimulate the formation of diamond-like structure, reducing the temperature requirements [43].

From Fig. 3(a), the negligibly low-intensity and wide sub-band is also seen at 288.49 eV (left part of the spectrum). This sub-band appeared in C 1s core-level spectrum due to the formed O–CO bond. It should be concluded that only weak oxidation happened for doped carbon and the majority of it remains in sp2 C–C chemical bonding. This also means that oxygen sublattice of MgAl2O4: C sample remains almost unaffected by carbon-doping process. Thus, XPS O 1s core-level spectra of pure MgAl2O4 nanoceramic and MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite will be nearly shape-identical because of the absence of bond deviations. The identity of XPS O 1s core-level spectra is clearly visible in Fig. 3(c) thus giving good coincidence with above reported C 1s core-level spectrum analytics.

The electronic states of the cations were also analyzed. Fig. 3(d) shows Al 2p core-level spectra, comparing it with that for combined native AlxOy – Al-metal [37]. XPS Al 2p core-level spectrum of MgAl2O4: C has no asymmetry in shape what is typical for an aluminum containing oxide compounds [36,37,39]. Also the spectrum of MgAl2O4: C almost perfectly match with that for pure MgAl2O4. But there is essentially dissimilarity from core-level spectra of combined native AlxOy – Al-metal and MgAl2O4. This means that aluminum is not subject to reduction in the synthesis process. In addition, there are no significant changes in alumina-oxygen bonding of AlO6 octahedrons as a result of the addition of carbon. Oxygen octahedra are the basic structural unit that includes the aluminum cation. At the same time, there is a possibility that magnesium cations exhibit a greater interaction with carbon atoms under thermobaric exposure.

XPS Mg 1s core-level spectra of combined MgO – Mg-metal XPS External Standard [36], pure MgAl2O4 and MgAl2O4: C are shown in Fig. 3(e). As in the case of Al 2p at the core-level, there is a discrepancy between Mg 2p position of both the pure and carbon-doped nanoceramics with the position of the metal standard and simple magnesium oxide MgO. The most perfect match is well recognized except near 1305 eV value of binding energy. In this case Mg 1s spectrum of MgAl2O4: C exhibits some extra width. We believe that little amount of magnesium-carbon-oxygen compound during carbon-doping might be appearance. Such magnesium-carbon-oxygen compound really exists and it is magnesite MgCO3 which exhibits XPS Mg 1s signal exactly at 1305 eV [36,39]. Thus we suppose that the fabrication of magnesite MgCO3 under carbon-doping with the high probability can be responsible for the mentioned broadening of Mg 1s for MgAl2O4: C compared with that for MgAl2O4. In order to inspect our supposition, we performed standard deconvolution of Mg 1s spectrum for MgAl2O4: C into its constituent components using XPS spectrometer in-built Avantage Software ver.5.9921 (see green and pink curves in Fig. 3(e)). We have to note that, whereas “deconvolution 1“ perfectly match the majority of MgAl2O4 Mg 1s signal, the very weak and broad “deconvolution 2“ has binding energy position at 1305.09 eV which well coincides 1305 eV Mg 1s signal from magnesite MgCO3.

XPS Valence band mapping of MgAl2O4: C sample in comparison with that for pure MgAl2O4 is shown in Fig. 3(f) [36,39]. From this figure well seen that no essential difference occurs after carbon-doping of MgAl2O4-host, only weak transformations of intensity happened in the area of 18–15 eV. This transformation of intensity was recognized as an influence of C 2s electronic states tail after carbon doping because it is completely absent in carbon undoped MgAl2O4-host. Also the transformations of Valence Base-band (VBB) area are absent. The majority of VBB is linked with Mg 3s electronic states because of photoionization cross-section relation under Al Kα excitation as σ(Mg 3s) : σ(O 2p) = 3.9 : 2.4 [44]. Almost complete identity of Valence bands for MgAl2O4: C and MgAl2O4 well proves XPS O 2p core-level spectra analytics presented above that no deviations of oxygen electronic states occur and even the fabrication of magnesite MgCO3 under carbon-doping has minor influence on the electronic structure of MgAl2O4: C. Also this conclusion well coincides with C 1s core-level spectrum analysis according to which the doped carbon almost did not interact with oxygen sublattice of MgAl2O4-host during carbon-doping.

Phase analysis of synthesized MgAl2O4: C composite shows the presence of both cubic MgAl2O4 and graphite modification of carbon (see Section 2.1, Table 1). The detection of graphite is not surprising due to the tendency of graphene particles to form large agglomerates. The individual graphene nanoplates can be formed as a result of the rupture of weak Van der Waals forces between nanoplates in agglomerate, and can be carried out, for example, by dispersion in an alcohol solution. Another method that allows to break such bonds is the mechanical shift of graphene plates. Such a scenario, in our opinion, should be realized as a result of grinding and mixing spinel nanopowder with carbon nanopowder in a sapphire mortar, as well as in the process of thermobaric synthesis. A significant limitation in the reliable detection of the graphene phase by XRD is its small amount per unit volume of sample. As shown in the previous section, the analysis of XPS spectra indicates the formation of carbon inclusions with both graphite and diamond structures. To clarify the nature of the CC bond, Raman spectroscopy was applied (Fig. 4 ).

The Raman spectrum of MgAl2O4: C is represented by a superposition of bands with maxima at 1058, 1230, 1340, 1500, 1593, 1614 and 1726 cm−1. The 1058 cm−1 mode (so-called T-peak) is due to C—C sp3 vibrations [45,46]. The peak at 1230 cm−1 (S-peak) is associated with breathing modes of rings containing seven or more carbon atoms [47]. High–intensity peak at 1340 cm−1 (D-peak) is due to the breathing modes of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in rings [47]. The band at 1500 cm−1 arises from amorphous carbon with sp2 bonds [48]. The band around 1593 cm−1 (G-peak) is assigned to bond-stretching motion of pairs of C sp2 atoms [49,50]. The nature of peak 1614 cm−1 (D’-peak) is related with the C–C stretching vibrations on the armchair and zigzag edge of graphene [51–53]. The peak at 1726 cm−1 is due to the CO stretching modes [54]. The nature of Raman bands observed for MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite is summarized in Table 2 . This data indicate that carbon is stabilized mainly in graphite modification in the sample under study. At the same time, there are small inclusions of diamond and amorphous carbon. The presence of diamond modification of carbon was also detected by the XPS analysis (see Section 3.1).

It is interesting to note that the D’- peak is quite intense in the Raman spectrum (Fig. 4). Since the nature of this band is related with stretching vibrations of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms on the armchair and zigzag edges of graphene plates, we assume the formation of small-sized carbon clusters, which are characterized by a high surface-to-volume ratio. The average diameter of graphite clusters
L a
can be estimated on the basis of an empirical expression using the ratio of intensity of d- and G-peaks [55,56]:
(3)
L a
= 44
I ( D )
I ( G )
,
( Å )
,

For the studied MgAl2O4: C composite, the average size of carbon clusters was estimated to be
L a
≈
40
Å
. The results obtained are consistent with the HRSEM data (S8). Thus, based on the analysis of XPS and Raman spectroscopy data, it can be concluded that graphite clusters and small inclusions of diamond and amorphous carbon are formed in the MgAl2O4: C nanocomposite. Insignificant oxidation of the surface of carbon clusters (formation of OC = O bonds) is associated with a feature of synthesis implying a top-down method [57] and a weak interaction of carbon with the aluminum-magnesium spinel host.

Optical absorption spectra of composites MgAl2O4: C with different carbon content are shown in Fig. 5 (a). Composites are transparent in the visible and IR spectral ranges. The nanoscale grains of the composites cause strong light scattering, which is reflected in the exponential increase in optical density with an increase in photon energy (dotted lines in Fig. 5(a)) [58,59]. This scattering is associated primarily with grain boundaries in the host, but we do not exclude that this scattering is partially caused by carbon nanoparticles [60]. A wide absorption band in the range of 4.0–5.5 eV is observed for the studied composites. Our previous studies showed that absorption in this spectral range for MgAl2O4 nanoceramics is due to the optical transitions in F- and F+ - centers [61]. Such centers are intrinsic defects (oxygen vacancies with localized electrons) of MgAl2O4 host, having a characteristic energy structure within the band gap. The absorption bands of F- and F+ - centers in MgAl2O4 are typically located at 5.3 eV (FWHM = 0.35 eV) and at 4.75 eV (FWHM = 0.32 eV), respectively [61]. A detailed analysis of a wide UV absorption for the studied MgAl2O4: C composites show the appearance of two additional bands at 4.1 eV (FWHM = 0.35 eV) and 5.0 eV (FWHM = 0.4 eV), as indicated in Fig. 5(b). These absorption bands are characteristic of π → π* and σ → π* optical transitions in sp 2-hybridized carbon domains [62,63]. In the previous sections, the analysis of XPS and Raman spectroscopy data confirmed the existence of sp2-hybridized carbon inclusions in the studied samples. Thus, UV absorption in MgAl2O4: C composite is caused by optical transitions both in intrinsic defects of MgAl2O4 host and in embedded CDs.

The concentrations of optically active centers were estimated by Eq. (2) and given in Supplementary Materials (S5). Calculations have shown that the concentration of CDs in MgAl2O4: C (10000:1) composite is at least 1.8 × 1016 cm−3. With an increase in the content of carbon impurity by an order of magnitude (MgAl2O4: C 10000:10 composite), the concentration of CDs increases by approximately 3 times (N = 6.2 × 1016 cm−3). At the same time, it should be noted that these are only estimates, and the real concentration may be higher (since the oscillator strength of the optical transitions is not actually equal to 1).

The study of photoluminescence properties was performed for a composite with a high carbon content (MgAl2O4: C 10000:10). Photoluminescence and excitation spectra are shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. 7
. The emission is observed both in the UV (3.4 eV–3.9 eV) and visible (2.1 eV–3.1 eV) spectral ranges. The luminescence band in the UV range is non-elementary and is represented by a superposition of two Gaussians (as shown by red and blue on the top panel in Fig. 7). We suppose, the UV emission is related with radiative transitions in two types of centers – intrinsic defects of MgAl2O4 host (F - centers) and CDs. In the case of CDs, the UV emission can be observed due to the π* → σ transition [63].

In the visible spectral range, more complex emission spectra are registered. In fact, we see a wide non-elementary luminescence band from blue to red range. Note that F-centers in MgAl2O4 aren't characterized by optical transitions in visible range, so we tend to assume that visible emission in MgAl2O4: C composite is related with CDs. A distinctive feature is that when the excitation energy changes, a synchronous shift of the emission maximum occurs (as shown by the arrows in Fig. 7). Currently, two of the most recognized mechanisms are proposed to explain excitation-dependent luminescence in CDs [14,63–66]. The first one assumes that the emission is caused by the radiative recombination of an electron-hole pair in the core of sp 2-clusters (π* → π transition). In this case, the spectral positions of emission and excitation bands are determined by the size of the cluster. With an increase in the size of sp 2-clusters, the HOMO-LUMO energy gap decreases, which causes a redshift of luminescence. In other words, this mechanism is based on a size effect that provides tunable photoluminescence. An alternative luminescence mechanism is based on the concept of optical transitions involving surface defect states. The surface defect states are generalized to include various chemical groups derived from sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbons, oxygen-related groups (surface oxidation), as well as dangling bonds on the armchair and zigzag edges. As a rule, surface defects are not characterized by discrete energy levels, but have continuous energy states, which provides excitation-dependent luminescence [67]. It is also important to note that in the case of a size effect (according to the first mechanism described above), the luminescence of CDs is usually represented by a set of narrow bands with clearly defined maxima, as was observed, for example, in our previous work [62]. On the contrary, for MgAl2O4: С nanocomposite, a wide spectrum of photoluminescence is observed (Fig. 7). In addition, an intense D’–band in the Raman spectrum (see Fig. 4, Section 3.2) indicates a high density of defective states originated from dangling bonds on the armchair and zigzag edges of sp 2-clusters. So, we tend to believe that excitation-dependent luminescence of CDs in MgAl2O4: C composite is related with radiative transitions involving surface defect states.

The decay kinetic of СDs emission at 2.21 eV (560 nm) at pulsed laser excitation at 3 eV (405 nm) is shown in Fig. 8 . The decay curve is well described by single exponent and the lifetime of the excited states is determined to be 4.7 ± 0.1 ns. Observed nanosecond time of excited states is typical for CDs [62–66].

The nanoceramics obtained in this work are characterized by optical transparency and attractive luminescent characteristics. The work demonstrates the conceptual possibility of synthesizing a new functional material with stable carbon dots embedded in the volume, having a tunable luminescence. The prospects of this approach are significant, and even at the current stage it is clear that there is a possibility of both unifying the embedded carbon dots in size for targeted modification of optical characteristics (for example, using the approach described in [68]), and improving the quality of the optical characteristics of the host (for example, improving the homogenization of the particles of the initial nanopowder). Another way for development is the embedding of sensitizers (rare earth ions or ions of the iron group) to increase PL intensity. This opens up prospects for further development of research in the direction of creating new light-emitting materials with carbon dots.

The optically transparent MgAl2O4: C nanocomposites has been successfully synthesized by the method of thermobaric synthesis using aluminum-magnesium spinel and carbon nanopowders as precursors. The analysis of XPS and Raman spectroscopy data shows that carbon clusters and small inclusions of diamond and amorphous carbon are formed in the MgAl2O4: C nanocomposites. The average size of carbon clusters was determined to be 40 Å. It should be noted that the obtained materials are characterized by a weak interaction of carbon with the oxygen sublattice of the oxide host. This feature is provided due to relatively low synthesis temperatures and contributes to the stabilization of carbon inclusions in a solid-state system. In the optical absorption spectra of the MgAl2O4: C nanocomposites, the bands associated with optical transitions in the intrinsic defects of the host (F - centers) and carbon dots were registered. The luminescence of carbon dots was observed both in UV and visible spectral ranges. A clear dependence of the spectral position of emission maximum on the excitation energy is due to the dispersion of energy states of defects on the surface of carbon dots. Thus, the thermobaric synthesis technology used in the work makes it possible to stabilize carbon dots in solid-state matrices, which is of interest for the development of light-emitting devices.

Arseny Kiryakov: Conceptualization, Resources, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yulia Kuznetsova: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Dhanushkodi Sivaganesh: Investigation. Tatiana Dyachkova: Conceptualization, Investigation. Chufarov Aleksandr: Investigation. Jeevaraj Murugan: Investigation. Alexander Tyutyunnik: Conceptualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The work was supported by the Russian Science Foundation grant No. 23-72-01024, https://rscf.ru/project/23–72–01024/ and from the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Russian Federation (Ural Federal University Program of Development within the Priority-2030 Program) as well as the state assignment for the Institute of Solid State Chemistry of the Ural Brunch of Russian Academy of Sciences (АААА-А19-119031890025-9).

The authors thank V.A. Volkovich for FTIR spectroscopy; the UCSU "Modern Nanotechnologies" (Institute of Natural Sciences and Mathematics, UrFU) for XPS measurements; the "Metalab" laboratory (Institute of Physics and Technology, UrFU) for access to spectroscopic equipment; Ju.V.Shchapova and Center for Collective Use Geoanalyst IGG UB RAS for Raman investigation.
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.apmt.2024.102067.
